title: Anger at God for the Slow Disappearance
dek: *When the person you love forgets your name incrementally, when your own strength drains month by month, when the life you expected evaporates so slowly you can't point to the day it died - rage at the divine sits in your tissues, waiting for permission to speak its truth about abandonment that arrives on cat feet.*
bodyMarkdown:
TL;DR. Anger at God for slow disappearances often hides beneath depression, fatigue, or numbness because gradual losses feel too diffuse to name. Your body holds the protest even when your mind refuses permission to feel betrayed by the divine. Recognition and expression of this anger can restore vitality and honest relationship with whatever you call sacred.

She sat in the hospital cafeteria at two in the afternoon, third day this week, stirring lukewarm coffee she wouldn't drink. Her father upstairs couldn't remember whether he'd had breakfast. Yesterday he knew her face but not her name. The month before, he'd told the same story three times in an hour, each telling fresh to him, each listening exhausting to her. She felt tired. Profoundly, bone-deep tired. If you'd asked her whether she was angry, she would have said no - she'd say she was sad, or worn out, or just coping. But her jaw ached from clenching. Her shoulders sat up near her ears. And somewhere beneath the fatigue, beneath the patient daughter performance, a question burned that she wouldn't let herself ask: *Where are you in this? Why won't you stop it?* ## The Particular Cruelty of Gradual Loss Sudden tragedy gives you a clear target. The diagnosis that comes back malignant, the accident that changes everything in a moment, the phone call in the night - these create a before and an after, a cliff edge where you can stand and rage at the sky with your whole throat. But slow disappearance works differently. The parent who fades across years. The marriage that cools one degree per season until you wake up strangers. The body that loses capacity so gradually you can't remember when you last ran, last made love without pain, last slept through the night. Your own dreams that shrink and shrink until you can barely recall what you wanted at twenty-five. These losses arrive like weather, like erosion. Each day looks almost like the one before. You adapt. You adjust. You tell yourself this is just life, just aging, just how things go. And because there's no single moment of rupture, there's no clear permission to grieve, much less to rage. In our experience working with people navigating these slow erasures, the anger doesn't disappear just because it lacks a convenient target. It goes underground. It becomes the depression that won't lift. The anxiety that spikes at nothing. The exhaustion no amount of sleep repairs. The body keeps the score of every incremental theft, even when the mind refuses to name the thief. And sometimes - often - the thief we won't name is God. The divine. The universe. Whatever force we believed was supposed to hold us, protect us, or at least make sense of suffering. When loss comes slowly, it feels less like tragedy and more like abandonment. Less like a storm and more like being quietly, persistently ignored. ## Why We Don't Let Ourselves Feel Betrayed by the Sacred You probably learned early that anger at God isn't allowed. Not in those words, anyway. Religious communities might permit "struggling with faith" or "wrestling with doubt," but raw fury at the divine for breaking promises? For rigging the game? For watching your beloveds disappear and doing nothing? That gets labeled as spiritual immaturity. Lack of faith. Ingratitude for blessings. The subtle message: your anger reveals your failure to understand divine mystery, not a legitimate response to abandonment. Even if you left organized religion years ago, that prohibition often remains. You might not call it God anymore - maybe it's the universe, or karma, or the fundamental benevolence you want to believe in. But the rule stays the same: you're allowed to be sad about hard things, but you're not allowed to feel betrayed by the structure of existence itself. Beverly Engel's work on anger and shame helps illuminate why this prohibition cuts so deep. She describes how anger, when met with shaming responses, doesn't disappear but instead turns inward, becoming self-attack or numbness. When the ultimate authority figure - the divine - is off-limits for your rage, that fury has nowhere to go but into your own body, your own sense of defectiveness. So you feel tired instead of angry. Depressed instead of furious. You wonder what's wrong with you that you can't accept what is, can't find peace with the slow vanishing of everything you love. You don't recognize that acceptance without anger is just dissociation with a spiritual marketing campaign. ## What the Body Knows About Divine Abandonment Your tissues tell a different story than your theology. Pat Ogden's somatic psychology research reveals how the body organizes around incomplete defensive responses - the fight or flight that never got to complete, the protest that got silenced, the reaching that met emptiness. When God is slowly taking something from you - a person, a capacity, a future you counted on - your body wants to fight. Not metaphorically. Actually. Your muscles want to push against the loss, your voice wants to shout *no*, your whole organism wants to refuse this unacceptable thing. But you can't fight the divine. You can't push back against dementia or aging or the basic structure of impermanence. So the fight response stays trapped. Your shoulders harden. Your jaw locks. Your breathing gets shallow. Your gut clenches. The anger becomes armor, becomes chronic tension, becomes the pain you bring to physical therapy without ever connecting it to the grief you won't let yourself feel about how God is letting your world disappear. Notice right now, as you read this: where are you holding tension? Let your attention drop into your shoulders, your belly, your jaw. What happens if you imagine letting yourself feel angry - not at a person, not at a situation, but at the divine itself for the way this is going? Does your body brace harder, or does something soften? In my own practice, I've found that the places we hold chronic tension often map directly to the angers we've forbidden ourselves. The tight throat protects the scream we won't release. The clenched belly guards the grief-rage at being abandoned by whatever we thought was supposed to keep us safe. ## The Difference Between Bitterness and Clean Anger You might worry that letting yourself feel furious at God will make you bitter. That it will poison your capacity for joy, for gratitude, for whatever relationship with the sacred you've managed to maintain. This fear confuses two different things: clean anger and toxic resentment. Clean anger moves. It has a beginning, a middle, and an end. It says clearly what it needs to say and then releases. It restores relationship rather than destroying it, because real relationship requires the truth of what you actually feel. Bitterness is what happens when anger can't move. When you can't let yourself feel it directly, so it seeps into everything. When you smile and say you're fine while your body keeps score of every betrayal. When you perform gratitude while privately despairing that the universe is fundamentally hostile or indifferent. Karla McLaren's work on emotional flow helps here. She describes anger as a boundary-setting emotion that arises when something important to you is being threatened or violated. Its job is to help you restore right relationship - to say no, to push back, to demand better. When slow disappearance is happening, anger's message is clear: *This matters to me. This person matters. This capacity matters. This future I imagined matters. And you're taking it anyway.* That's a true statement, not a sinful one. Saying it out loud - even to God, especially to God - doesn't damage relationship. It makes relationship real. The alternative is the fake intimacy of always agreeing, always accepting, always performing serenity while your actual self is furious and heartbroken and feels utterly abandoned. That's not spiritual maturity. That's just good behavior driven by fear. ## A Practice for Speaking the Unspeakable You don't need a formal ritual for this. You don't need to be in a sacred space or wait for the right moment. You just need five minutes of privacy and a willingness to tell the truth. Find a place where you won't be interrupted. If you want, light a candle - not because the divine needs candlelight, but because your nervous system might need a signal that this is set-apart time. Speak out loud. Not in your head, out loud. Address whatever name you use for the sacred: God, Universe, Divine Mother, the Mystery, Life Itself. And tell the truth about what you're angry about. *I'm angry that you're taking my father's mind piece by piece. I'm furious that I have to watch him disappear while you do nothing. I'm enraged that this is slow enough that I have to be patient and graceful and strong for months, for years, while my heart breaks incrementally.* Don't perform. Don't try to sound spiritual or mature or balanced. Let your voice shake. Let yourself cry. Let the words be messy and raw and maybe even cruel. This isn't about being fair to God. This is about your body finally getting to say what it's been holding. If you need to move, move. Pound a pillow. Push against a wall. Let the anger be physical, not just verbal. Your body needs to complete the protest response it's been holding in check. And here's the surprising part: most people report that after they let themselves rage at God, they feel closer to the divine, not farther. Because real relationship can hold your fury. Fake relationship requires your silence. ## When Anger Reveals What You Actually Believe Across the articles we've published on this site, one pattern shows up consistently: the angers you're most afraid to feel reveal the beliefs you're most afraid to examine. If you're terrified to be angry at God for slow disappearance, what does that fear tell you about what you actually believe? Maybe you believe God is fragile and can't handle your rage. Maybe you believe love is conditional and fury will cost you divine protection. Maybe you believe suffering is a test and anger means you're failing. These aren't abstract theological positions. They're the working theories your nervous system operates from, the ones that shape whether you let yourself feel what you feel or perform what you think you should feel. Getting angry at God for the slow vanishing of what you love forces the question: what do you actually believe about the nature of the sacred? Is the divine big enough to hold your fury? Strong enough to stay in relationship when you stop pretending everything is fine? If your God can't survive your honest rage, you might need a bigger God. Not a different religion necessarily, but a more robust understanding of what the sacred actually is. The divine that needs your constant approval and gratitude isn't divine - it's just a narcissistic parent projected onto the cosmos. The God worth talking to is the one who can handle being told: *You're breaking my heart. You're letting terrible things happen to people I love. I don't understand you and I'm furious with you and I need you to show up differently.* That conversation is the beginning of real relationship, not the end of it. ## The Metabolic Cost of Unfelt Rage Your body pays for the anger you won't let yourself feel. The APA's research summary on anger management documents the physiological toll of suppressed anger: elevated blood pressure, compromised immune function, increased inflammation, disrupted sleep, chronic muscle tension. When the anger is specifically at the divine for slow loss, the cost compounds. You're not just suppressing an emotion - you're maintaining a constant performance of acceptance, of faith, of being okay with what's happening. That performance requires tremendous energy. In traditional Chinese medicine, suppressed anger is understood to stagnate liver qi, creating a cascade of physical symptoms: digestive issues, headaches, menstrual irregularities, muscle tension, irritability that seems to come from nowhere. The herbs in formulations like [Plum Flower Four Marvel Teapills (Si Miao Wan, 200 ct)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B004ZH5ROI?tag=spankyspinola-20) are sometimes used to address the heat and dampness that practitioners associate with long-held anger affecting the body's metabolic processes. Similarly, Ayurvedic traditions recognize that unexpressed emotion creates ama - toxic residue in the tissues. Cooling, clarifying herbs like those in [Banyan Botanicals Brahmi/Gotu Kola Tablets (alt)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B077RZW18Q?tag=spankyspinola-20) are sometimes used to support mental clarity when the mind is clouded by emotions that haven't been fully processed. But no herb can substitute for the actual expression of what you feel. Your body needs you to tell the truth - to God, to yourself, to someone who can witness - about the fury you're carrying over these slow thefts. The exhaustion you feel isn't just from caregiving or from watching loss unfold. It's from the constant muscular effort of holding back your rage. It's from the metabolic cost of maintaining the lie that you're at peace with what's happening when you're actually being torn apart by it. ## The Anger That Restores Aliveness Here's what nobody tells you about anger at God for slow disappearance: it's a sign of life. It means you still care. It means you haven't numbed out completely. It means your heart is still breaking, which means your heart is still open. The real spiritual danger isn't rage at the divine. It's the cold acceptance that comes when you've given up expecting anything to change. It's the flat affect of someone who's stopped protesting because protest seems pointless. It's the depression that settles in when anger has nowhere to go. Over the years I've seen people come back to life through their anger. Not despite it - through it. The woman who finally screamed at God for her daughter's addiction and then, for the first time in months, felt something other than numb. The man who raged at the universe for his wife's dementia and discovered that his fury was actually a form of love - a refusal to pretend this was okay. Your anger says: this matters. This loss is real. This person is precious. This disappearance is not acceptable, even if it's inevitable. That protest, that refusal to be okay with unbearable things, is what keeps your heart alive through the long erosion. When you support your body through this process - whether through movement, through herbs like [MegaFood Balanced B Complex (30 tabs)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B00028ONPI?tag=spankyspinola-20) that support the nervous system during stress, through therapy, through practices that help you metabolize emotion - you're not fixing the anger. You're giving it room to move, to speak, to complete its message. And its message is always the same: you are alive. You love. You refuse to go gently into acceptance of the unacceptable. That refusal is sacred, even when it's directed at the sacred itself. ## FAQ: Common Questions About Anger at the Divine ### Is it sinful to be angry at God? No. Anger at God is documented throughout sacred texts - the Psalms are full of it, Job is essentially one long argument with the divine, mystics across traditions have raged at heaven. What's sinful, if anything, is the pretense that you're not angry when you are. Your relationship with the sacred, like any real relationship, requires honesty. God can handle your fury. The question is whether you can handle letting yourself feel it. ### What if my anger at God makes me lose my faith? Faith that can't survive your honest feelings isn't faith - it's just good behavior motivated by fear. Real faith is tested and deepened by anger, not destroyed by it. Many people report that after they let themselves rage at God for slow losses, their relationship with the divine becomes more real, more intimate, more grounded in truth rather than performance. You might lose certain beliefs about God, but you'll gain a relationship that can hold all of who you are. ### How do I know if I'm angry at God or just angry in general? Pay attention to the specific content of your rage. If you find yourself furious about the basic structure of existence - why things have to disappear, why bodies fail, why love can't protect people from harm - that's anger at the divine order of things. If you notice yourself having imaginary arguments about fairness, about promises broken, about being abandoned or ignored by whatever force is supposed to care, that's anger at God. It often shows up as existential rage rather than situation-specific frustration. ## Opening to What Anger Protects The slow disappearance continues whether you're angry about it or not. Your father will keep forgetting. Your body will keep changing. The future you imagined will keep receding. Rage doesn't stop any of that. But rage does something else. It keeps your heart involved. It says that this matters, that you're not resigned, that you refuse to make peace with unbearable things just because they're happening anyway. That refusal is a form of love - messy, painful, fully alive love that won't pretend everything is fine. Underneath the anger, if you let yourself feel it all the way through, you'll find the grief. The pure, clean heartbreak of loving in a world where everything you love disappears. And underneath the grief, you might find something else: a fierce tenderness toward your own heart for keeping on loving anyway. For staying open even when openness means being torn apart by slow loss. The anger isn't the endpoint. It's the doorway. On the other side is a relationship with the sacred that's real enough to hold your fury, your heartbreak, and your refusal to go numb. That's not the spirituality they sell you in books or teach you in churches. It's wilder than that, more honest than that. It's what happens when you stop performing acceptance and start telling the truth about what it costs to love what you're losing, one slow day at a time. For more resources on working with difficult emotions, visit [the assessments hub](/assessments) or explore [more articles in this journal](/articles). You can also find complementary perspectives at [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com), and learn more about our approach on [the about page](/about). ---
Written by **The Oracle Lover**, independent essayist on anger, somatics, and embodied spirituality. Last updated May 29, 2026. In our work on anger at god for the slow disappearance, we keep returning to one quiet truth: the body has been telling you something for a long time, and it deserves a thoughtful listener. The Anger Practice is a companion journal to [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com), and every piece here is written with you in mind.
